messages.content: You are a Lean 4 autoformalization agent. Generate a small ordered sequence of Lean declarations for the provided LaTeX theorem-like unit. Use only the source text, selector plan, previous-project context if shown, and Lean-checked Mathlib signatures in the prompt. The original aligned Lean declarations, names, statements, and proofs are withheld. Return exactly one JSON object.

{
  "benchmark_policy": {
    "gold_comparison_is_posthoc_only": true,
    "posthoc_alignment_hidden": true,
    "target_lean_available_to_generator": false
  },
  "instructions": [
    "Do not use sorry, admit, placeholders, or comments standing in for proof.",
    "Do not include import statements or markdown fences.",
    "Do not ask for or infer the hidden aligned Lean declaration names/statements/proofs.",
    "Treat selector_unchecked_statement_sketch as non-authoritative mathematical intent only; do not copy its Lean syntax verbatim.",
    "Follow the Lean-checked signatures exactly when they differ from the selector's expected shape.",
    "Use the actual Lean-checked argument order from hydrated_mathlib_context. If the selector expected shape conflicts with the checked signature, the checked signature wins.",
    "If a selected Mathlib API is field-specific, unit-specific, or otherwise not strong enough for the source statement, say so in notes and generate only declarations justified by the visible context.",
    "Do not invent project helper names that are not shown in available_prior_project_context, needed_project_context, or source context.",
    "When available_prior_project_context contains Lean snippets for project definitions or predicates, use those exact names and statement shapes instead of rephrasing the source with raw hypotheses.",
    "Prefer a narrow declaration sequence over a broad bundled conjunction when the source unit decomposes into multiple Lean declarations.",
    "Keep theorem-local assumptions explicit rather than relying on unavailable global variables.",
    "If local_file_context_candidates show useful namespace, open, notation, or variable commands, you may include the needed commands in lean_file_body before the declarations.",
    "If you use a variable command instead of explicit binders, the command itself must appear in lean_file_body. Do not depend on file-scope variables that are not emitted in your output.",
    "Every identifier used in a theorem statement must be introduced by an explicit binder, local declaration, or imported/opened declaration. In particular, source parameters such as coefficient types, matrix sizes, rings, variables, and typeclass assumptions must be bound in the generated theorem, e.g. `{K : Type*} [CommRing K] {n : ℕ}` when the source quantifies over a commutative ring and a natural number.",
    "If you cannot produce a complete proof from visible context, set status to cannot_prove_from_visible_context and leave lean_file_body empty. Never output Lean containing sorry/admit/placeholders."
  ],
  "lean_environment": {
    "default_opens_used_for_context_hydration": [
      "open scoped Polynomial BigOperators",
      "open PowerSeries Finset"
    ],
    "imports_available_in_verification_file": [

    ],
    "toolchain": "leanprover/lean4:v4.28.0"
  },
  "required_json_schema": {

        ],
        "lean_file_body": "complete Lean declarations only; no imports or markdown",

          "brief caveats or remaining uncertainty"
        ],
        "status": "generated|cannot_prove_from_visible_context",
        "unit_key": "unit-001",
        "used_context": [
          "source/project/Mathlib facts actually used"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },

            "source_text": "\\begin{corollary}\n\\label{cor.det.sig-row-col}Let $n\\in\\mathbb{N}$. Let $A\\in K^{n\\times n}$ and\n$\\tau\\in S_{n}$. Then,%\n\\begin{equation}\n\\det\\left(  \\left(  A_{\\tau\\left(  i\\right)  ,j}\\right)  _{1\\leq i\\leq\nn,\\ 1\\leq j\\leq n}\\right)  =\\left(  -1\\right)  ^{\\tau}\\cdot\\det A\n\\label{eq.cor.det.sig-row-col.row}%\n\\end{equation}\nand%\n\\begin{equation}\n\\det\\left(  \\left(  A_{i,\\tau\\left(  j\\right)  }\\right)  _{1\\leq i\\leq\nn,\\ 1\\leq j\\leq n}\\right)  =\\left(  -1\\right)  ^{\\tau}\\cdot\\det A.\n\\label{eq.cor.det.sig-row-col.col}%\n\\end{equation}\n\n\\end{corollary}"
          }

        "source_text": "\\begin{theorem}\n[Multiplicativity of the determinant]\\label{thm.det.detAB}Let $n\\in\\mathbb{N}%\n$. Let $A,B\\in K^{n\\times n}$ be two $n\\times n$-matrices. Then,%\n\\[\n\\det\\left(  AB\\right)  =\\det A\\cdot\\det B.\n\\]\n\n\\end{theorem}"

                  "lean_snippet": "/-- Elementary symmetric polynomials are symmetric.\n    Label: def.sf.ehp -/\ntheorem e_isSymm (n : ℕ) : IsSymm (e (K := K) (N := N) n) := esymm_isSymmetric (Fin N) K n\n\n/-!\n## Complete Homogeneous Symmetric Polynomials (Definition def.sf.ehp (b))\n\nh_n = sum of all monomials of degree n\n    = ∑_{i₁ ≤ i₂ ≤ ... ≤ i_n} x_{i₁} x_{i₂} ⋯ x_{i_n}\n\nIn Mathlib, this is `MvPolynomial.hsymm (Fin N) K n`.\n-/\n\nsection WithDecidableEq\n\nvariable [DecidableEq (Fin N)]",

            ],
            "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/SymmetricFunctions/Definitions.tex",
            "referenced_labels": [],
            "source_text": "\\begin{definition}\n\\label{def.sf.ehp}\\textbf{(a)} For each $n\\in\\mathbb{Z}$, define a symmetric\npolynomial $e_{n}\\in\\mathcal{S}$ by%\n\\[\ne_{n}=\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i_{1},i_{2},\\ldots,i_{n}\\right)  \\in\\left[\nN\\right]  ^{n};\\\\i_{1}<i_{2}<\\cdots<i_{n}}}x_{i_{1}}x_{i_{2}}\\cdots x_{i_{n}%\n}=\\left(  \\text{sum of all squarefree monomials of degree }n\\right)  .\n\\]\nThis $e_{n}$ is called the $n$\\emph{-th elementary symmetric polynomial} in\n$x_{1},x_{2},\\ldots,x_{N}$. \\medskip\n\n\\textbf{(b)} For each $n\\in\\mathbb{Z}$, define a symmetric polynomial\n$h_{n}\\in\\mathcal{S}$ by%\n\\[\nh_{n}=\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i_{1},i_{2},\\ldots,i_{n}\\right)  \\in\\left[\nN\\right]  ^{n};\\\\i_{1}\\leq i_{2}\\leq\\cdots\\leq i_{n}}}x_{i_{1}}x_{i_{2}}\\cdots\nx_{i_{n}}=\\left(  \\text{sum of all monomials of degree }n\\right)  .\n\\]\nThis $h_{n}$ is called the $n$\\emph{-th complete homogeneous symmetric\npolynomial} in $x_{1},x_{2},\\ldots,x_{N}$. \\medskip\n\n\\textbf{(c)} For each $n\\in\\mathbb{Z}$, define a symmetric polynomial\n$p_{n}\\in\\mathcal{S}$ by%\n\\begin{align*}\np_{n}  &  =%\n\\begin{cases}\nx_{1}^{n}+x_{2}^{n}+\\cdots+x_{N}^{n}, & \\text{if }n>0;\\\\\n1, & \\text{if }n=0;\\\\\n0, & \\text{if }n<0\n\\end{cases}\n\\\\\n&  =\\left(  \\text{sum of all primal monomials of degree }n\\right)  .\n\\end{align*}\nThis $p_{n}$ is called the $n$\\emph{-th power sum} in $x_{1},x_{2}%\n,\\ldots,x_{N}$.\n\\end{definition}"
          }
        },

            "source_text": "\\begin{example}\n\\label{exa.sf.ehp.1}\\textbf{(a)} The $2$-nd elementary symmetric polynomial is%\n\\begin{align*}\ne_{2}  &  =\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i_{1},i_{2}\\right)  \\in\\left[  N\\right]\n^{2};\\\\i_{1}<i_{2}}}x_{i_{1}}x_{i_{2}}=\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i,j\\right)\n\\in\\left[  N\\right]  ^{2};\\\\i<j}}x_{i}x_{j}\\\\\n&\n\\begin{array}\n[c]{cccccccc}%\n= & x_{1}x_{2} & + & x_{1}x_{3} & + & \\cdots & + & x_{1}x_{N}\\\\\n&  & + & x_{2}x_{3} & + & \\cdots & + & x_{2}x_{N}\\\\\n&  &  &  & \\ddots & \\cdots & \\cdots & \\cdots\\\\\n&  &  &  &  &  & + & x_{N-1}x_{N}.\n\\end{array}\n\\end{align*}\n\n\n\\textbf{(b)} The $2$-nd complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial is%\n\\begin{align*}\nh_{2}  &  =\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i_{1},i_{2}\\right)  \\in\\left[  N\\right]\n^{2};\\\\i_{1}\\leq i_{2}}}x_{i_{1}}x_{i_{2}}=\\sum_{\\substack{\\left(  i,j\\right)\n\\in\\left[  N\\right]  ^{2};\\\\i\\leq j}}x_{i}x_{j}\\\\\n&\n\\begin{array}\n[c]{cccccccccc}%\n= & x_{1}^{2} & + & x_{1}x_{2} & + & x_{1}x_{3} & + & \\cdots & + & x_{1}%\nx_{N}\\\\\n&  & + & x_{2}^{2} & + & x_{2}x_{3} & + & \\cdots & + & x_{2}x_{N}\\\\\n&  &  &  &  &  & \\ddots & \\cdots & \\cdots & \\cdots\\\\\n&  &  &  &  &  & + & x_{N-1}^{2} & + & x_{N-1}x_{N}\\\\\n&  &  &  &  &  &  &  & + & x_{N}^{2}.\n\\end{array}\n\\end{align*}\n\n\n\\textbf{(c)} The $2$-nd power sum is%\n\\[\np_{2}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\\cdots+x_{N}^{2}.\n\\]\nIn light of the above two formulas for $e_{2}$ and $h_{2}$, we thus have\n$h_{2}=p_{2}+e_{2}$. \\medskip\n\n\\textbf{(d)} We have%\n\\[\ne_{1}=h_{1}=p_{1}=x_{1}+x_{2}+\\cdots+x_{N}.\n\\]\n\n\n\\textbf{(e)} We have%\n\\[\ne_{0}=h_{0}=p_{0}=1.\n\\]\n\n\n\\textbf{(f)} If $n<0$, then $e_{n}=h_{n}=p_{n}=0$. \\medskip\n\n\\textbf{(g)} If $N=0$ (that is, if $\\mathcal{P}$ is a polynomial ring in $0$\nvariables), then $e_{n}=h_{n}=p_{n}=0$ for all $n>0$ (because there are no\nmonomials of positive degree in this case), but $e_{0}=h_{0}=p_{0}=1$ still\nholds. This is a boring border case which, however, is important to get right.\n\\end{example}"

        "source_text": "\\begin{proposition}\n\\label{prop.sf.en=0}For each integer $n>N$, we have $e_{n}=0$.\n\\end{proposition}"
